context: because they have not hearkened to my word, saith the Lord, which I sent unto them by my servants, the prophets, rising up early and sending them, but ye would not hear, saith the Lord.
point_summary: Jeremiah 29:19 is cited to show that Judah's impending Babylonian captivity, like Israel's Assyrian exile, resulted not from ignorance of God's word but from willful refusal to hear and obey it.

context: Hear ye therefore the word of the Lord, all ye of the captivity whom I have sent from Jerusalem to Babylon.

context: Yet my people have forgotten me days without number

context: return now backsliding Israel

context: Take your Bible and turn with me to, if you will, please, Jeremiah chapter 33.

context: Call unto me, and I will answer thee.

context: 'Call unto me, and I will answer thee and show thee great and mighty works or mighty things which thou knowest not.'

context: there's nothing too hard for him

context: am I a God at hand, saith the Lord, and not a God afar off?
point_summary: God's omnipresence means no one can hide from Him in any secret place, as His rhetorical question in Jeremiah 23:23–24 declares that He fills heaven and earth.

context: Can any hide himself in secret places that I shall not see him, saith the Lord? Do not I fill heaven and earth, saith the Lord?
point_summary: God's omnipresence means no one can hide from Him in any secret place, for He fills heaven and earth — a truth meant here as comfort, not only conviction.

context: call unto me and I will answer thee. Show thee great and mighty things which thou knowest not
point_summary: God's standing invitation to call upon Him—cited from Jeremiah 33:3—is woven into the closing prayer as assurance that the Shepherd-God hears and answers His people.

context: He loves us with an everlasting love
point_summary: God's everlasting love for his people, drawn from Jeremiah 31:3, grounds the assurance that the Lord is a shepherd who does not flee but remains faithfully present.

context: The heart is deceitful above all things and desperately wicked. Who can know it?
point_summary: Human hearts are inherently deceitful and wicked, making the popular counsel to "follow your heart" a direct contradiction of Scripture.

context: The Lord tries the heart
point_summary: God alone searches and knows the heart, making popular advice to "follow your heart" directly contrary to Scripture's declaration that the heart is deceitful above all things.

context: call unto me and I will answer thee and show thee great and mighty things which
point_summary: The Hebrew word for "call" in this verse, used over 50 times in Psalms alone, underscores that God's people have always had the distinctive privilege of crying out to a God who actually hears and answers.

context: flip over quickly to Jeremiah chapter 51
point_summary: Jeremiah 51:15, though not using the term Elohim, is cited to reinforce that God's creative power and wisdom—themes embedded in that name—are affirmed throughout Scripture.

context: he hath made the earth by his power. He hath established the world by his wisdom and hath stretched out the heaven by his understanding
point_summary: Jeremiah 51:15 is cited not for its use of Elohim but to reinforce that God's creative power—making the earth, establishing the world, stretching out the heavens—is a truth attested beyond Genesis 1.

context: Look at the book of Jeremiah, if you will, in Jeremiah chapter 32
point_summary: Just as Hezekiah prayed through crisis by invoking Elohim as creator and strong one, Jeremiah likewise turns to God's revealed name amid the imminent Babylonian captivity, demonstrating that biblical saints prayed according to the attributes embedded in God's names.

context: behold, Hanamel, the son of Shalom, thine uncle shall come unto thee saying, buy thee my field that is in Anathoth for the right of redemption is thine to buy it
point_summary: God's command to Jeremiah to purchase a field on the eve of Babylonian captivity illustrates that divine instruction can run counter to visible circumstances, grounding obedient action in revealed promise rather than present reality.

context: God is going to reveal to him in verse number 14
point_summary: Jeremiah's purchase of a field on the eve of Babylonian captivity, preserved in an earthen vessel for many days, is cited as evidence that God's revealed names and attributes grounded prophetic obedience even amid imminent judgment.

context: thus saith the Lord of hosts, the God of Israel, take these evidences, this evidence of the purchase, both which is sealed and this evidence which is sealed and this evidence which is open and put them in an earthen vessel that they may continue many days. For thus saith the Lord of hosts, the God of Israel, houses and fields and vineyards, notice what he says, shall be possessed again in this land
point_summary: God's command to preserve the purchase deed in an earthen vessel, paired with the name Elohim, signals His eternal authority to declare the end from the beginning—promising restored possession of the land beyond the coming captivity.

context: now when I had delivered the evidence of the purchase unto Barak the son of Neriah, I prayed unto the Lord saying
point_summary: Jeremiah's prayer in verse 16, opening with God as Creator of heaven and earth, reflects his grasp of the name Elohim—the same name God used in commanding him to buy the field.

context: ah, Lord God, behold, thou hast made the heaven and the earth by thy great power and stretched out arm and there is nothing too hard for thee
point_summary: Jeremiah's prayer in verse 17 grounds petition in creation, invoking God's power over heaven and earth as the basis for declaring nothing too hard for Him.

reference_text: there is nothing too hard for thee
context: and notice what he says, and there is nothing too hard for thee
point_summary: Jeremiah's declaration that nothing is too hard for God is grounded in the name Elohim—the powerful creator who made everything from nothing—giving him confidence to obey even when the command seemed humanly inexplicable.

context: as Jeremiah said, there's nothing too hard for thee
point_summary: Knowing God by His specific name Elohim—the strong Creator—grounds the believer's confidence that nothing is too hard for Him, as Jeremiah confessed.

context: Call unto me, and I will answer thee, and show thee great and mighty things, which thou knowest not
point_summary: Jeremiah 33:3's promise that God will answer when called is confirmed by Psalm 99:6's historical record of Moses, Aaron, and Samuel calling and receiving God's answer, together establishing that prayer works.

context: call unto me and I will answer thee and show thee great and mighty things which thou knowest not
point_summary: God's ability to do "great and mighty things" (Jer. 33:3) is invoked to reinforce that what seems impossible to men—here, water in a dry wilderness—is "but a light thing in the sight of the Lord."

context: Look at Jeremiah chapter 3
point_summary: God's offer to "heal your backslidings" in Jeremiah 3:22 extends the divine healer motif beyond physical disease to the spiritual restoration of a rebellious, idol-following people.

context: here in Jeremiah chapter number 3
point_summary: God's offer to "heal your backslidings" in Jeremiah 3:22 frames spiritual restoration as a form of divine healing, extended to a rebellious, idol-following people on the eve of Babylonian captivity.

context: return ye backsliding children and I will heal your backslidings. Behold, we come unto thee for thou art the Lord our God
point_summary: God's promise to "heal your backslidings" in Jeremiah 3:22 is presented as evidence that divine healing extends beyond the physical to the spiritual—restoring those who have drifted from closeness with God.

context: my people would not hearken to my voice
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: can a maid forget her ornaments or a bride her attire? Yet, notice what he says, yet my people, my people have forgotten me days without number
point_summary: Judah's indictment of forgetting God "days without number" illustrates that forgetting God is not an intellectual lapse but a practical one—trusting self and following one's own way instead of the Lord.

context: a voice was heard upon the high places weeping and supplications of the chosen of Israel for they have perverted their way and they have forgotten the Lord, their God
point_summary: Jeremiah 3:21 is cited alongside Jeremiah 2:32 as evidence that Judah's indictment was practical, not merely intellectual, forgetting of God—living self-directed lives while nominally retaining knowledge of him.

context: No thing is too difficult for Thee
point_summary: God's unlimited ability to do the impossible—"no thing is too difficult for Thee"—grounds the sermon's call to long expectantly for works that can only be explained as God's own doing.

context: We find that Jeremiah in chapter number 30 refers to this day of
point_summary: Jeremiah 30:7 identifies the tribulation period as "the time of Jacob's trouble," establishing that Daniel's 70th week is entirely about Israel, not the church.

context: the time of Jacob's trouble in Jeremiah 30 and verse number 7
point_summary: Jeremiah's phrase "time of Jacob's trouble" identifies the tribulation as a period specifically concerning Israel, since Jacob's name was changed to Israel.

context: Why does Jeremiah refer to it as the time of Jacob's trouble?
point_summary: Jeremiah 30:7's designation of the Tribulation as "the time of Jacob's trouble" is cited as evidence that this period belongs to Israel, not the church.
